title: Manakish with Oregano and Sesame (Zaatar-Style Flatbread)

subs.name: whole wheat flour (atta) for a denser, healthier base
subs.notes: add 1 extra tablespoon water as atta absorbs more

subs.name: skip yeast and use 0.5 tsp baking powder — the bread will be flatter but still tasty
subs.notes: allow no resting time with baking powder

ingredients.name: dried oregano
visual_checks: zaatar spice mix is traditional; dried oregano plus sesame is a good home substitute
ingredients.notes: zaatar spice mix is traditional; dried oregano plus sesame is a good home substitute

subs.name: dried thyme, or a mix of thyme and dried mint
subs.notes: dried mint plus sesame is a common household shortcut

ingredients.name: sumac or amchur powder (optional)
visual_checks: adds the tangy note of authentic zaatar blend
ingredients.notes: adds the tangy note of authentic zaatar blend

steps.text: Mix yeast, sugar and warm water in a small bowl. Leave for 5 minutes until frothy — this confirms the yeast is active.

Combine flour and salt in a bowl. Add the yeast water and 1 tablespoon olive oil. Knead for 6–8 minutes into a smooth, slightly tacky dough. Cover with a damp cloth and rest for 20 minutes in a warm spot.
While the dough rests, mix the dried oregano, sesame seeds, amchur (if using) and 3 tablespoons olive oil together into a thick paste — this is the zaatar-style topping.
Divide the dough into 4 equal balls. On a lightly floured surface, roll each ball into a round about 18 cm across and 3 mm thick.
Spread 1 tablespoon of the herb-sesame paste evenly over each flatbread, almost to the edges.

Heat a dry tawa on medium-high flame. Place a flatbread topping-side up and cover with a lid. Cook for 3–4 minutes until the base is golden and the topping is fragrant and set. Repeat for all flatbreads.
Serve warm. Manakish is traditionally eaten folded or rolled with fresh tomato, cucumber and white cheese — or simply eaten plain as a morning snack.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Fermentation conditions affect safety. Discard batches with unexpected mould, putrid odour or other spoilage signs.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: maida → whole wheat flour (atta) for a denser, healthier base (add 1 extra tablespoon water as atta absorbs more); oregano → dried thyme, or a mix of thyme and dried mint (dried mint plus sesame is a common household shortcut); yeast → skip yeast and use 0.5 tsp baking powder — the bread will be flatter but still tasty (allow no resting time with baking powder)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Tawa-cooking works perfectly — no oven or pizza stone needed. Dried oregano (ajwain phool or regular oregano) is available in grocery stores and online. Sesame seeds (til) are a kitchen staple in India. Amchur powder is a great Indian substitute for sumac's tartness.
